title: Morning Bid: Metals meltdown
content:
LONDON, Feb 2 (Reuters) - By Marc Jones, Global Markets Correspondent

What matters in U.S. and global markets today

The slump in gold, silver and other precious metals is continuing to rattle global markets and making for a nervous start to the month. After Kevin Warsh’s nomination as the new Fed Chair on Friday, a potent mix of margin calls, momentum selling and - at least for now - a dialing down of U.S.-Iran sabre-rattling appears to be fueling ongoing volatility in the one part of the market most prized for its safety.

There is a lot of red on the screens out there again and that is ahead of a week packed with corporate earnings, central bank meetings and major economic data, starting with ISM numbers out later.

I’ll get into all the market news below.

But first, check out Mike Dolan’s latest column below on why the Warsh-Trump pairing may not be a match made in heaven.

And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast. Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

METALS MELTDOWN

Having zoomed up on the escalator, the worry is now that gold and silver markets are hurtling down in the lift as investors unwind their trades. Silver is currently down another 5.5% and after Friday’s 30% plunge is headed for its biggest two-day loss since at least the 1980s. Gold has dropped another 3.4% too after its 9% Friday meltdown marked its biggest single-day decline since 2013.

Dealers say pressure on a number of silver futures funds in China added to the rout late last week and the mood hasn’t been helped today by news that CME had raised margin calls on some of its key gold and silver futures contracts.

Adding to the commodity crunch, oil prices are also down nearly 5% after Trump said over the weekend that Iran was "seriously talking" with Washington, perhaps lessening the risk of a U.S. military strike on the country, at least in the coming days.

It has all started to knock the equity markets. Wall Street’s Nasdaq futures are off nearly 1% while the so-called VIX "fear gauge" is rising toward the 20 level again.

About one quarter of the S&P 500 is set to report this week, and growth in earnings per share was running at 11% on the previous year, when consensus had been for 7%. Scrutiny will be razor sharp on tech majors Alphabet, Amazon and AMD, particularly on their costs and how they see the benefits of AI in the wake of Microsoft’s poorly received results last week.

European stocks are showing some signs of stabilisation - around 30% of the STOXX 600’s constituents report earnings this week. But Asia saw the "bubbly" South Korean stock market tumble 5.5% as its big name chipmakers got whacked by the persistent AI jitters and Hong Kong’s Hang Seng also fell over 2% after data over the weekend revealed China’s official PMI fell back below 50 again. Japan’s Nikkei also dropped more than 1% despite a brief push into the green on an opinion poll suggesting Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s Liberal Democratic Party was likely to score a landslide victory in next week’s lower house election.

The coming week also has a deluge of U.S. macro data. Monday kicks things off with the January ISM manufacturing index although the main event will be Friday’s non-farm payrolls report, with consumer sentiment figures and the latest Treasury’s quarterly refunding details keeping the interest up in between.

Also on the menu are policy meetings by the Reserve Bank of Australia, European Central Bank and Bank of England.

The RBA is an outlier in that markets imply around a 75% chance it will raise interest rates by a quarter point to 3.85%, so reversing one of three cuts delivered last year, in an attempt to quell resurgent inflation.

Chart of the day

It’s worth acknowledging that despite the severity of the gold and silver routs, they remain comfortably higher than the already lofty positions at which they started the year.

They raced higher when Washington captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro and then a major row blew up with Europe about Trump’s proposed grab for Greenland.

While that rally has hit the buffers for now, it could just be a timely correction. Deutsche Bank on Monday said it still saw the case for a gold price of $6,000 an ounce given the uncertain global outlook and European defence stocks are also still firmly in vogue.

On the flip side, the dollar has risen for the last couple of days as Warsh’s nomination for the Fed job has eased some of the more extreme worries about the U.S. central bank’s cherished independence. And its strength - or lack of it - remains crucial for all kinds of markets.

Today’s events to watch * U.S. January ISM index (10:00 AM EST) * Canada January manufacturing PMI (9:30 AM EST) * Fed’s Bostic speaks, BoE’s Breeden speaks * U.S. corporate earnings: Palantir, Walt Disney

Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles, is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here.

(By Marc Jones; Editing by Sharon Singleton)

title: Morning Bid: Metals get a lot less precious as positions squeezed
content:
A look at the day ahead in European and global markets from Wayne Cole

Someone shouting fire in a crowded theatre is what’s happening in the metals market. Everybody and their mum was piling into gold and silver - retail investors and leveraged funds, algos, CTAs and momentum plays. So when silver falls 30% in one session, it’s everyone for the exits.

The CME has reacted by raising margins on several metals contracts by between 2 and 4 percentage points, suggesting some investors are having trouble meeting margin calls.

Dealers also say Chinese investors in popular silver futures funds were having trouble liquidating positions, with selling spilling over into other markets. Momentum is a powerful force, but it works in both directions.

Silver was down, up, and now down again by around 8% at $78.50 an ounce. It was still just above Friday’s trough of $73.70, though that may not be true by the end of this sentence, so volatile is trading.

That has made for a risk-off tone in equities where all of Asia is in the red. Even the Nikkei could not sustain early gains made when opinion polls pointed to a majority win by the LDP in upcoming lower house elections.

It was notable that neither gold nor Treasuries picked up a safe-haven bid amid the losses, suggesting investor confidence in U.S. assets is less than sound.

The rout in gold is pressuring other "bubbly" markets, led by South Korean stocks with a drop of 5.5%. Wall Street futures are off 1.2% or so, and European futures between 0.6% and 1%.

This means corporate earnings will have to be all the better to justify stretched valuations. About one quarter of the S&P 500 is reporting this week, along with around 30% of Euro STOXX market capitalisation. So far, S&P 500 EPS growth is running at 11% y/y against consensus forecasts for +7%.

Obviously, all eyes will be on tech majors Alphabet, Amazon and AMD, particularly for the costs and benefits of AI in the wake of Microsoft’s badly received results.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs noted consensus estimates for AI hyperscalers’ capex this year had climbed to $561 billion, up 38% on 2025 and compared to $540 billion expected at the start of earnings season.

Key developments that could influence markets on Monday:

- Data include global PMIs, U.S. ISM survey for January

- Speeches by BoE Deputy Governor Sarah Breeden, Executive Director Rebecca Jackson

- Appearance by Fed Bank of Atlanta President Raphael Bostic

(By Wayne Cole; Editing by Sonali Paul)

title: Heavy earnings week, jobs data to test US stocks after Microsoft swoon
content:
By Lewis Krauskopf

NEW YORK, Jan 30 (Reuters) - Another huge batch of corporate earnings including from megacaps Alphabet and Amazon will test the U.S. stock market in the coming week after a disappointing report from heavyweight Microsoft weighed on equity indexes.

Wall Street also will focus on the monthly U.S. jobs report due on February 6. This week, the Federal Reserve pointed to signs of stabilization in the labor market as the U.S. central bank paused its interest rate-cutting cycle.

With the stock market entering the fourth year of a bull market, investors have been wary of rising valuations, particularly for high-flying names benefiting from optimism over artificial-intelligence-driven profits.

Microsoft, which has spent massively on infrastructure to support AI applications, saw its shares battered on Thursday after its cloud business failed to impress, while software shares were broadly punished amid further disappointment elsewhere in the industry.

"For those companies where expectations have become very, very lofty, the onus is going to be on them to deliver," said Jim Baird, chief investment officer with Plante Moran Financial Advisors. "Even if they show growth, if it is growth that is not up to the expectations of the market, there is a risk there that their stock price could be punished."

Despite declining at the end of the week, the benchmark S&P 500 remained up over 1% for the year and not far from record-high levels. The index earlier in the week broke above the 7,000 level for the first time, before pulling back.

Investors in the coming days will continue to digest a series of developments on Friday, including President Donald Trump’s nomination of former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh to be the central bank’s next chair. Stunning declines in prices of gold and silver on Friday, following huge climbs for the precious metals, also kept markets on edge.

BIG EARNINGS WEEK ON TAP

About one quarter of the S&P 500 is set to report quarterly results in the coming week, with strong expected U.S. profit growth a key source of optimism underpinning bullish outlooks for equities in 2026.

Of 166 S&P 500 companies that reported results as of Friday, 76.5% posted earnings above analysts’ expectations, nearly in line with the 78% rate over the prior four quarters, according to LSEG IBES. Fourth-quarter earnings are expected to have climbed 10.9% from a year earlier.

In contrast to Microsoft, Meta Platforms -- another megacap company and major AI spender -- posted strong sales in its quarterly report that boosted its shares on Thursday.

Investors will now focus on results and capital spending plans from Google parent Alphabet and Amazon, two other AI-focused "hyperscalers."

"Although investor reaction to earnings announcements from a couple of the hyperscalers was mixed, it did confirm that capex spending on building out AI infrastructure will not see any letup," said Sid Vaidya, chief investment strategist at TD Wealth.

Other companies set to report next week include weight-loss drugmaker Eli Lilly, chipmaker Advanced Micro Devices and media giant Walt Disney. S&P 500 companies overall are expected to increase earnings by 15% in 2026, putting their financial outlooks under the microscope.

"The stock market is largely reflecting the positive fundamentals that are driving that, and earnings growth is the biggest component of that," Vaidya said.

A STABLE JOBS MARKET?

The coming week’s jobs report will also give Wall Street a critical look at the economy’s health. The nonfarm payrolls report for January is expected to show growth of 64,000, according to a Reuters poll.

Data flow is normalizing following the lag effects from the 43-day government shutdown late last year that delayed key economic reports. The monthly consumer price index, closely watched for inflation trends, is due the following week.

"We haven’t really gotten a lot of clean looks at the state of the labor market and inflation because of that government shutdown last year, so we think those are going to probably be more important than usual," said Michael Reynolds, vice president of investment strategy at Glenmede.

Following Wednesday’s Fed meeting, markets are now pricing in the central bank to hold off on further rate cuts until its June meeting, although any surprise weakening in the labor market could sway those expectations.

"The broad sense is that the economy is on a decent growth trajectory here going forward, and I would expect that that alone should help to provide a little bit of a floor under payrolls," Baird said.

title: 5 big analyst AI moves: Apple faces ’further downside’; ASML, AMAT upgraded
content:
Investing.com -- Here are the biggest analyst moves in the area of artificial intelligence (AI) for this week.

Nvidia pullback is overdone, analyst says

The recent weakness in Nvidia shares looks out of step with the strength of near-term fundamentals, according to Morgan Stanley, which said investors remain focused on why the stock has lagged in a “very robust AI environment.”

Analyst Joseph Moore said the team has been “somewhat surprised at Nvidia’s underperformance YTD after a weak close to 2025,” adding that one of the most frequent investor questions is what has driven the pullback and how those overhangs could clear in 2026.

He said “checks remain very strong, and getting stronger,” while optimism around earnings is already building across the market. Moore noted he is increasingly hearing “references to $9+ in earnings power this year, vs. consensus $7.75,” which he said makes near-term upside “highly likely.”

Several of the concerns weighing on sentiment appear overstated, in the analyst’s view. Moore pointed to the fact that “the number of AI beneficiaries are broadening out” as demand accelerates and supply constraints ripple across the industry.

Some investors are also focused on financing issues tied to frontier model developers and “Nvidia’s role in that financing,” which Moore said will require “some adjustment” in how the market interprets the risk.

Meanwhile, worries about rising competition from custom ASICs and AMD continue to surface but are “overblown,” according to the analyst.

Looking ahead, Moore highlighted the upcoming Vera Rubin platform as a major catalyst that should provide a “strong demonstration of NVIDIA’s leadership” and help ease concerns around share momentum.

“The bottom line is that we see the stock outperforming from here,” he wrote, noting that while Nvidia still faces a “wall of worry to climb,” the company appears well positioned to move beyond it.

Apple faces fresh margin pressure as NAND costs surge, Lynx warns

Apple may be heading into a sharper profitability squeeze than the market is pricing in, according to the brokerage firm Lynx Equity Strategy, which sees “further downside” for the shares even after their roughly 10% decline so far this year.

The firm said its channel checks point to rising memory costs, with Apple now facing “the unpleasant prospect of an abrupt spike up in NAND flash pricing” following a breakdown in talks with long-time supplier Kioxia.

Lynx wrote that “bad blood” has developed after Kioxia’s lower long-term-agreement pricing for Apple created a “margin shortfall” at the supplier. As a result, Kioxia “may be shipping to Apple less than Apple’s forecasted demand,” forcing the company to look elsewhere for supply.

That shift could prove costly. Lynx analysts said Apple has been “forced to approach Samsung to fill the supply shortfall,” and without long-term contracts in place, Samsung is “free to quote current market price,” potentially at much higher levels.

An overnight Taiwan report cited by Lynx suggests Samsung “may have ratcheted up NAND pricing by as much as 100%,” with Apple likely among the impacted customers.

Beyond higher costs, Lynx also flagged technical concerns. Apple’s flash controller is “optimized for Kioxia’s NAND process,” and “will not work well with Samsung’s NAND process,” raising the risk of performance problems and possible product returns.

“The Street is underestimating the impact,” Lynx said, warning that both margins and the stock could remain under pressure in the period ahead.

ASML lifted at Barclays on record orders, more upside room

ASML shares climbed earlier this week after Barclays upgraded the stock to Overweight, citing surging orders, accelerating AI-led demand and upside scenarios it believes are still not fully priced in.

“Expectations were high heading into results but the hope materialised into record orders which drives material upgrades to estimates,” analyst Simon Coles said.

Barclays lifted its price target to €1,500 from €1,200, arguing there remains “scope for further upside” as ASML’s guidance appears conservative despite strengthening fundamentals.

The company reported record order intake of €13.2 billion, nearly double last year’s level and well ahead of expectations. It also raised its 2026 revenue outlook to €34 billion–€39 billion, above consensus forecasts and stronger than its prior view for largely flat growth versus 2025.

Alongside the upbeat demand picture, ASML announced plans to cut around 1,700 jobs as part of a broader restructuring aimed at streamlining management while expanding engineering capacity.

Barclays said lithography demand tied to large-scale data-center buildouts remains a core driver, while additional upside could come from consumer AI adoption, humanoid robotics and a more durable memory spending cycle.

Foundry competition was flagged as especially supportive, with Coles pointing to “upside risk from this competition driving increasing investment which should benefit ASML/semicap into 2027.”

Meanwhile, China concerns were also seen as overdone. “These seem unnecessary for 2026 with ASML sensibly setting guidance that implies China down more than 10% yoy to start but recent import strength would suggest demand remains strong,” Coles wrote.

The analyst now expects low-teens revenue growth in 2026 and 2027, driving mid-to-high-teens earnings upgrades, with further upside possible if AI investment and foundry spending accelerate faster than expected.

Mizuho upgrades Applied Materials on rising chip equipment spending

Applied Materials was upgraded to Outperform from Neutral by Mizuho on Wednesday, as the firm pointed to a sharp rebound in semiconductor capital spending and improving demand across foundry and logic customers.

Analyst Vijay Rakesh hiked the price target on the stock to $370 from $275, saying the company is set to benefit from a “significant acceleration” in wafer fab equipment (WFE) spending through 2027.

Mizuho now expects WFE growth of 13% year over year in 2026, followed by another 12% in 2027, which represents a “significant acceleration versus our prior expectations.”

With roughly 65% of revenue tied to foundry and logic customers, Rakesh flagged rising capital outlays from TSMC and improving tool spending at Intel as key drivers of growth.

TSMC’s capital expenditures between 2026 and 2028 are projected to be “significantly higher” than in the 2023–25 period, with 2026 spending alone seen jumping 32% to $54 billion.

Memory demand is also expected to provide support. Rakesh said DRAM tied to high-bandwidth memory (HBM) should remain strong, representing about 30% of Applied Materials’ revenue base.

While China continues to act as a drag, with the company’s China revenue forecast to fall 4% this year, the analyst said the rest of the world — about 70% of sales — is accelerating at a faster pace. He added that AI-driven investment is pushing leading-edge development at nodes below 2 nanometers.

Rakesh said the broader pickup in global WFE spending is creating “strong tailwinds for all WFE suppliers,” underpinning its upgrade and higher estimates for 2026 and 2027.

BofA keeps Snowflake as top software pick, sees AI fueling faster growth

Bank of America (BofA) said Snowflake remains “one of the fastest growing stories in software,” reaffirming its Buy rating and maintaining it as a top pick within infrastructure software.

Analyst Koji Ikeda said the bank views Snowflake as well-positioned to benefit from accelerating enterprise spending on data analytics and AI, given its role as a core data cloud platform.

In a note to clients, Ikeda said the central question for investors is whether Snowflake can sustain product revenue growth in the “high 20%s Y/Y” or potentially “reaccelerate into the 30s.” He argued this outcome is “well within reach,” pointing to a broader product suite, strong AI-related demand and increasing customer usage.

“The blizzard is just starting to form on broad AI adoption, and we think Snowflake will play a foundational role,” he wrote.

BofA expects Snowflake’s growth trajectory to remain “top tier in infrastructure software,” significantly ahead of peers expanding at roughly 10%.

The bank also raised its price target to $275, saying it refreshed its valuation framework “for our updated view on growth, risks, peer multiple compression.”

Snowflake is “fast becoming the king of enterprise data in the cloud,” Ikeda said, pointing to its OLAP data lakehouse architecture, which allows customers to scale compute and storage independently to improve performance and manage costs.

While acknowledging the stock is “not cheap,” with shares trading at a “123% premium” to peers, the analyst said the valuation looks more reasonable on a growth-adjusted basis, where Snowflake trades “at the same multiple.”

title: Investing.com’s stocks of the week
content:
Investing.com – There was no shortage of big-name stock moves this week, with earnings releases ramping up and significant gains in some tech names.

Here are Investing.com’s stocks of the week:

Despite rising for most of the shortened trading week, Intel reversed course on Friday, giving up those initial gains and more, down more than 17% as of 13:30 ET after the company’s guidance disappointed investors.

The company, whose stock price has recently surged thanks to fresh cash injections from major investors, including even the U.S. government, posted a net loss of $333 million for the final three months of its fiscal year, worse than Wall Street analysts had anticipated.

Executives flagged the impact of supply shortages caused by soaring demand from data centers, which house the chips that power cutting-edge AI models. For its first quarter, Intel said it now expects to slide to a loss of $0.21 per share.

“After gaining 150% over the TTM and nearly 50% since Jan 2, we believe Intel’s shares were poised for a pull-back in all but the most optimistic of scenarios, and while the company delivered a respectable upside to December expectations, its disappointing Q1 outlook provided more than enough reason for traders to sell shares aggressively,” wrote Benchmark analyst Cody Acree.

Abbott shares plunged more than 10% on Thursday and have declined a further 2.3% so far on Friday as investors reacted to its latest earnings release.  Shares are down around 13.8% over the last week.

The company missed revenue expectations, with nutrition sales declining 8.9%, which the company attributed to lower sales volumes and strategic price actions intended to drive future volume growth. Its guidance also disappointed.

“The majority of the top-miss driven by the Nutrition business,” said Piper Sandler analyst Adam Maeder. “Medtech growth of +10.4% ex-fx was nothing to thumb your nose at, though bears will pick on the Q4 diabetes number, which came in lighter than we/Street modeled. The FY26 revenue guide was below expectations driven entirely by nutrition (from what we can tell).”

Meanwhile, Qiagen shares surged, up over 13% in the last week, thanks to a more than 16% rally on Tuesday. It followed a Bloomberg report on Tuesday evening that indicated Qiagen is exploring strategic options.

The report said Qiagen is exploring strategic options, including a potential sale, following renewed takeover interest.

SanDisk’s inclusion in our Stocks of the Week list has been a regular occurrence over the last few months, given its significant rally.

In the last week, it has surged a further 11.4%, with the demand for memory continuing. Other names such as Micron, Western Digital, and Seagate Technology have also experienced strong gains.

Finally, AMD shares have performed well over the last week, climbing around 9.7%. The company’s stock price continues to benefit from the strong demand for artificial intelligence.

In a note on Wednesday, KeyBac analyst John Vinh reiterated an Overweight rating on the stock, telling investors that the firm expects AMD to post better results and higher guidance when it reports earnings, “driven largely by robust server CPU demand momentum and latest generation (Turin) strength.”

title: AMD stock rating reiterated at Overweight by Wells Fargo
content:
Investing.com - Wells Fargo has reiterated its Overweight rating on Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) with a price target of $345.00, representing a potential 43% upside from the current price of $240.56. This target falls within the analyst range of $210-$380, with the overall analyst consensus remaining bullish at 1.55 (Strong Buy).

The reaffirmation comes amid reports of potential delays in AMD’s MI450-series chips, which has put pressure on the company’s stock. AMD shares have retreated nearly 3% over the past week, though they still maintain impressive gains of 40.5% over the last six months and 112% year-over-year.

Wells Fargo analyst Aaron Rakers maintained his positive outlook on the semiconductor company despite these concerns circulating in the market.

The financial services firm indicated it expects AMD to address these rumors during next week’s announcements, potentially highlighting confidence in the MI450 production ramp.

AMD’s MI450-series represents the company’s next generation of accelerator chips designed for artificial intelligence and high-performance computing applications.

In other recent news, AMD has been at the center of several significant developments. Piper Sandler has raised its price target for AMD to $300, citing anticipated revenue and earnings growth, particularly for the December 2025 quarter. Meanwhile, KeyBanc continues to hold an Overweight rating on AMD, expecting the company to surpass consensus earnings due to strong demand for its server CPUs and the latest Turin processors. This positive sentiment is echoed by Aletheia, which questions if current forecasts for AMD’s server CPU business are too conservative, projecting a substantial revenue increase in fiscal 2026.

Additionally, Bernstein has adjusted its price target for AMD to $225, acknowledging the company’s advancements in artificial intelligence, although it notes that OpenAI is currently the only major announced customer for AMD’s Helios chips. In related news, BofA Securities has increased its price target for Sanmina-SCI Corp to $190, ahead of its fiscal first-quarter 2026 earnings. This adjustment is partly due to potential revenue generation from AMD rack builds as NVIDIA rack revenues decline. These updates provide a snapshot of the evolving landscape for AMD and its partners.

This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

title: AMD stock falls on MI450-series delay rumors
content:
Investing.com -- Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) stock declined Friday, falling as much as 6% before paring losses to around 3%, following reports that the company’s next-generation MI450-series AI accelerators might face delays.

The downward pressure came after SemiAnalysis reportedly claimed AMD’s MI450-series chips were behind schedule. However, Wells Fargo analyst Aaron Rakers quickly countered these assertions, suggesting the claims were unfounded based on his team’s research.

According to Rakers, AMD’s development on TSMC’s N2 (2nm) process technology remains on track, with the company having already completed tape-out and shipped Venice EPYC processor samples that use the same process node as the upcoming MI450-series. The N2 process is crucial for AMD’s next-generation AI accelerators.

Wells Fargo expects AMD to reaffirm its confidence in the MI450-series launch timeline during the company’s upcoming earnings report next week. The chipmaker has previously indicated that volume production would commence in the second half of 2026, with significant shipments weighted toward the fourth quarter of that year to customers including OpenAI and Oracle.

Despite Friday’s stock decline, Rakers maintained a bullish stance on AMD, suggesting investors view the weakness as a buying opportunity. The analyst anticipates AMD will report server revenue exceeding $38 billion and remains focused on the company’s path toward achieving more than $35 billion in data center GPU revenue by 2027.

title: Adv Micro Device receives Investment Bank Analyst Rating Update
content: PAID

title: BofA flags attractive compute valuations, highlights 4 key stocks
content:
Investing.com -- Bank of America said compute-focused chipmakers look increasingly attractive as valuations remain well below historical levels, even as artificial intelligence investment accelerates.

In a note on Monday, BofA described the semiconductor landscape as a mix of “stretched semicaps, compelling compute, [and] half-full analog,” with analyst Vivek Arya highlighting the relative value in four key compute names.

BofA said its preferred compute stocks, Nvidia, Broadcom, AMD and Credo, are projected to grow sales at an average 42% and adjusted EPS at 49% from 2025 to 2027, yet trade at “a 24x CY27 PE or just 0.5x PEG ratio.”

According to BofA, that “suggest[s] a notably compelling valuation backdrop” compared with other areas of the chip sector.

The firm expects large cloud providers to reaffirm “the mission-critical need to invest in compute infra,” supporting double-digit sales growth.

BofA also pointed to cloud spending momentum, noting its tracker suggests 38% year-over-year growth in 2026 cloud capex, with potential revisions “towards 50%+ exiting the year.” Despite rising investment, “FCF in aggregate is still positive,” BofA believes.

BofA “expects cloud AI earnings/NVDA’s GTC show (Mar 16-19) anticipation to reenergize the group.”

title: Piper Sandler raises AMD stock price target to $300 on AI prospects
content:
Investing.com - Piper Sandler has raised its price target on AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) to $300 from $280 while maintaining an Overweight rating on the stock.

The research firm expects AMD to deliver revenue upside of approximately $200 million for the December 2025 quarter, with potential to exceed that level, and earnings per share upside of at least $0.02 relative to consensus estimates.

For the March quarter, Piper Sandler sees potential for upside as well, though it expects the gains to be relatively small given seasonal aspects of client, gaming, and enterprise EPYC CPU business.

The firm believes AMD’s stock and fundamentals will "really start to shine" in mid-2026 as the company prepares to ramp up its Helios rack and its contract with OpenAI.

Between now and midyear, Piper Sandler expects several OpenAI partners and other customers to announce purchase commitments for AMD products.

In other recent news, KeyBanc has reiterated its Overweight rating and maintained a $270 price target on AMD, citing strong server CPU demand through 2026. The firm expects AMD to post better-than-consensus results in its upcoming fourth-quarter 2025 earnings report, driven by robust demand for its latest generation Turin processors. Meanwhile, Bernstein has raised AMD’s stock price target to $225, reflecting progress in the company’s artificial intelligence initiatives, although OpenAI remains the only major announced customer for AMD’s Helios chips. In a strategic move, AMD has appointed KC McClure, former CFO of Accenture, to its board of directors. McClure brings extensive experience in finance and accounting leadership, having served as a senior advisor to Accenture. On the other hand, BofA Securities has adjusted its price target for Sanmina-SCI Corp. to $190, while maintaining a Neutral rating. This adjustment comes as analysts anticipate the company’s fiscal first-quarter 2026 earnings report, focusing on revenue generation from AMD rack builds. Aletheia has also released a note suggesting that current forecasts for AMD’s server CPU business may be conservative, projecting a significant revenue increase in fiscal 2026.

title: Samsung to start HBM4 deliveries to Nvidia next month, reports show
content:
Investing.com-- Samsung Electronics (KS:005930) is set to begin shipping its next-generation HBM4 high-bandwidth memory chips to Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) in February, ahead of several rivals, South Korean media reported on Monday, citing industry sources.

The Korea Economic Daily said Samsung has passed qualification tests for its sixth-generation HBM4 with Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices and will start shipments next month, marking an early foray into the supply of advanced AI memory.

Samsung, long trying to catch up with market leader SK Hynix (KS:000660) in high-performance memory, plans production of HBM4 from next month, reports showed.

HBM4 chips are critical components for high-performance artificial intelligence accelerators, offering significant gains in data bandwidth and efficiency.

Seoul-listed Samsung shares traded flat at the time of writing, after rising as much as 2.8% to 156,400 won earlier in the day, just below record highs of 157,000 won.

title: Samsung to start production of HBM4 chips next month for Nvidia supply, source says
content:
SEOUL, Jan 26 (Reuters) - Samsung Electronics plans to start production of its next-generation high-bandwidth memory (HBM) chips, or HBM4, starting next month and supply them to Nvidia, a person familiar with the matter told Reuters on Monday.

The person declined to give details such as how many chips it plans to supply to Nvidia.

A Samsung spokesperson declined to comment.

South Korean newspaper Korea Economic Daily reported on Monday that Samsung passed HBM4 qualification tests for Nvidia and AMD and will start shipping to those companies next month, citing chip industry sources.

title: U.S.-China AI arms race increasingly becoming national security issue, Wolfe says
content:
Investing.com - Earlier this month, U.S. President Donald Trump rolled out a new 25% tariff on certain artificial intelligence chips, including Nvidia’s H200 AI processor and a rival model from Advanced Micro Devices.

The announcement, which came after a nine-month investigation into the semiconductor industry, was viewed as part of a wider effort to incentivize U.S. chipmakers to build more products domestically, rather than rely on foreign companies, particularly in Taiwan.

Officials from the White House later clarified that the tariffs will be narrowly focused, adding that they will not apply to chips and other devices imported for use in U.S. data centers -- many of which house the high-end processors which power AI models. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick will also have broad discretion around other exemptions, according to the announcement from the Trump administration.

The move comes after Trump vowed to place levies on imports of Chinese semiconductors, although he postponed the order until June 2027. The president also said last year that he would allow Nvidia to export its H200 chips to China in exchange for a portion of the sales -- but questions have surrounded whether this would be in violation of a constitutional ban on export tariffs.

Still, the White House’s posture suggests the AI race between the U.S. and China has increasingly become a national security issue, according to analysts at Wolfe Research.

In a note, the analysts including Stephanie Roth argued that this was underscored by the COVID-19 pandemic, when constraints in the flow of chips highlighted global dependence on steady supplies of these processors.

“Leadership in AI matters for technological leadership, military capability, and economic growth,” the analysts wrote.

They added that while the U.S. remains the “clear leader” in training the biggest and most capable AI models, China “has made progress largely through state-directed, capital-intensive policies.” Beijing has also sought to compete through efficiency, optimization and faster diffusion of “good-enough” models under hardware constraints, the analysts said.

title: UBS explains why ’nostalgia is not an investment strategy’
content:
Investing.com -- UBS Global Wealth Management’s chief investment officer has cautioned investors against clinging to the old international order as geopolitical rivalry reshapes markets, urging a shift toward diversification and broader equity exposure.

"Nostalgia for the old international order is not an investment strategy," Mark Haefele wrote in UBS’ monthly letter, paraphrasing Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney’s Davos speech.

The wealth manager highlighted a more volatile world marked by the US administration’s "Donroe Doctrine," strained China-Japan relations, Iranian violence, and NATO fractures over Greenland and Ukraine, all pointing to geopolitical blocs seeking greater strategic autonomy.

Despite the turbulence, UBS maintained a positive market stance, forecasting the S&P 500 to reach 7,700 by year-end from 6,876 currently, with earnings per share growth of 12% for 2026. The brokerage expects global equities to rise over 10% by end-2026.

The recent equity rally has been narrow, with the "AI7,” - NVIDIA, Broadcom, AMD, Micron, Alphabet, Amazon, and Microsoft - appreciating roughly 200% over three years, about five times the gain of the rest of the S&P 500, according to UBS data.

The brokerage expects leadership to shift from semiconductor firms toward companies selling AI solutions to consumers and businesses.

Global AI capital expenditures are forecast to rise at an average annual rate of 25% from 2025 to 2030, reaching $1.3 trillion per annum.

The Ramp AI Index, measuring US businesses with paid AI subscriptions, rose to 47% in December, roughly doubling from a year earlier.

UBS identified government intervention as a near-term risk, particularly around affordability. A Politico poll in late 2025 found 56% of Americans consider high living costs a top issue.

However, with 62% of American households owning stocks according to a 2025 Gallup poll, the brokerage believes the administration will avoid broad market-negative policies.

President Trump’s Davos comments suggesting Congressional action on credit card rate caps, rather than executive orders, signal limited appetite for intervention, UBS said.

The drive for strategic autonomy is creating beneficiaries across sectors. The US secured a $500 billion Taiwan investment deal, including $250 billion in semiconductors and AI capacity.

Europe’s Chips Act targets 20% global market share by 2030, while China’s Five-Year Plan includes four trillion yuan for grid investment, a 40% increase.

Defense spending is accelerating, with German outlays projected at 3.5% of GDP by decade-end and Trump calling for a $1.5 trillion 2027 military budget, up from $901 billion in 2026.

UBS upgraded US consumer discretionary stocks and emerging-market bonds to "Attractive," forecasting European earnings acceleration of 7% in 2026 and 18% in 2027 after three years of stagnation.

The brokerage noted geopolitical concerns have driven foreign holdings of US Treasuries lower while boosting gold, which it continues to favor alongside quality bonds for portfolio diversification in an increasingly fragmented world.

title: Sanmina price target raised to $190 from $180 at BofA on AMD potential
content:
Investing.com - BofA Securities has raised its price target on Sanmina-SCI Corp. (NASDAQ:SANM) to $190 from $180 while maintaining a Neutral rating ahead of the company’s fiscal first-quarter 2026 earnings report. The electronics manufacturer will face significant scrutiny as it navigates its relationship with AMD (NASDAQ:AMD), which is currently trading near its 52-week high with a remarkable 106% return over the past year.

BofA estimates that NVIDIA racks currently contribute approximately $1 billion per year to Sanmina’s revenue stream, creating pressure to develop new growth channels.

The investment bank expects the first half of calendar year 2026 will be dedicated to qualification and testing of AMD’s MI400 series racks, with meaningful revenue from these rack builds likely starting in the fourth quarter of 2026.

In other recent news, AMD’s server CPU business outlook has been bolstered by feedback from cloud providers, with Aletheia questioning whether current forecasts might be too conservative. Their research projects AMD’s server CPU revenue to reach $14.5 billion in fiscal 2026, a 42% increase from the anticipated $10.2 billion in fiscal 2025. KeyBanc has maintained its Overweight rating on AMD, citing strong demand for server CPUs and highlighting that AMD is nearly sold out through the end of 2026. The firm expects AMD to exceed consensus results in its upcoming fourth-quarter 2025 earnings report.

Bernstein has raised AMD’s stock price target to $225, reflecting progress in the company’s AI initiatives, although OpenAI is currently the only major announced customer for AMD’s Helios chips. Additionally, AMD has appointed KC McClure, former CFO of Accenture, to its board of directors, bringing extensive financial and accounting leadership experience. Meanwhile, Goldman Sachs has expressed a preference for Broadcom and Nvidia over AMD in the compute ecosystem, due to their ties to AI capital expenditure and advancements in networking technology. These developments highlight the ongoing shifts and strategic moves within the semiconductor industry.

title: AMD server CPU outlook strengthened by cloud provider feedback
content:
Investing.com - Aletheia has released a new research note questioning whether current forecasts for AMD’s (NASDAQ:AMD) server CPU business might be too conservative. The chipmaker’s stock has surged over 106% in the past year, currently trading at $253.73, near its 52-week high of $267.08, as investors bet on its continued growth trajectory.

The research firm’s January 5 update had established what it called a "constructive baseline" for AMD’s server CPU business, projecting fiscal 2026 revenue to reach $14.5 billion, representing a 42% year-over-year increase from the $10.2 billion anticipated in fiscal 2025.

This forecast was built on a projected 27% growth in unit sales and an 11% expansion in average selling price (ASP) for AMD’s server processors.

Aletheia now indicates that recent feedback from cloud service providers (CSPs) and memory suppliers suggests market conditions for fiscal 2026 may be even more robust than their current assumptions reflect.

The research note, titled "Are We Bullish Enough on Server CPU?", implies that AMD’s server business growth trajectory could potentially exceed the already substantial growth rates previously forecasted by the firm.

In other recent news, AMD’s upcoming earnings report has garnered attention from KeyBanc, which maintains an Overweight rating on the company with a price target of $270. The investment firm anticipates AMD will exceed consensus expectations, driven by robust demand for its server CPUs and the strength of its Turin processors. KeyBanc highlights that AMD is nearly sold out of server CPUs through 2026, with potential price increases on the horizon. Meanwhile, Bernstein has adjusted AMD’s price target to $225, reflecting progress in the company’s artificial intelligence initiatives, although OpenAI remains the only major customer for AMD’s Helios chips so far. Additionally, AMD has appointed KC McClure, former CFO of Accenture, to its board of directors, enhancing its leadership team. In other developments, Riot Platforms has signed a deal with AMD to host 25 MW of IT infrastructure at its Rockdale site, with options for expansion. This partnership is seen positively by Piper Sandler, which reiterated an Overweight rating on Riot Platforms. These recent developments underscore AMD’s strategic moves and ongoing industry engagement.

title: Surging memory chip prices dim outlook for consumer electronics makers
content:
By Jaspreet Singh and Zaheer Kachwala

Jan 22 (Reuters) - Global demand for smartphones, personal computers and gaming consoles is expected to shrink this year as companies from Britain’s Raspberry Pi to HP Inc raise sticker prices to offset surging memory chip costs.

The rapid build-out of artificial intelligence infrastructure by U.S. tech firms such as OpenAI, Alphabet’s Google and Microsoft has absorbed much of the world’s memory chip supply, which has lifted prices as manufacturers prioritize components for higher-margin data centers over consumer devices.

South Korea’s Samsung and SK Hynix, and Micron, the world’s three largest producers of memory chips, have all said that they were struggling to keep up with demand that boosted their quarterly earnings.

"Rising (memory) pricing is a dynamic we continue to watch closely, especially relative to the client market, and could limit our revenue opportunity this year," Intel CFO David Zinsner said on Thursday, referring to the company’s personal computer chip business.

Research firms IDC and Counterpoint both now expect global smartphone sales to shrink at least 2% this year, in a sharp reversal from their growth outlook a few months ago. That would mark the first annual decline in shipments since 2023.

The PC market is expected to shrink at least 4.9% in 2026, IDC estimated, after an 8.1% growth last year. Meanwhile, console sales are expected to fall 4.4% in the current year after an estimated growth of 5.8% in 2025, according to TrendForce.

TOUGH CHOICES FOR MANUFACTURERS

While several firms have already raised prices, industry heavyweights Apple and Dell face a tough choice: take on the costs and sacrifice margins or pass them onto consumers at the risk of stifling demand.

"Manufacturers might absorb some costs but given the scale of the shortage, it is certainly going to show up as higher prices for consumers," Emarketer analyst Jacob Bourne said.

"It is going to result in more tepid consumer device sales in 2026. It will be a challenge for these companies that are trying to sell products during a time of broader inflation."

Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan said on Thursday that while larger device manufacturers could get their hands on more memory chips from their suppliers, smaller businesses were scrambling. "... they are missing the memory, they cannot complete the products," Tan said on a post-earnings call.

The chipmaker forecast quarterly revenue and profit below market estimates on Thursday, sending shares down 13% in after-hours trading. Rival PC processor provider AMD’s shares also fell 1.2%.

Analysts believe the impact is likely to be most pronounced for manufacturers of low- and mid-range devices, such as Chinese smartphone makers Xiaomi and TCL Technology and PC firm Lenovo.

Pressure is being compounded by expectations that the price increases will persist, possibly into next year. Counterpoint estimates that memory prices will jump 40% to 50% in the first quarter, after last year’s 50% surge.

"Over the last two quarters, we’ve seen 1,000% price inflation in some products and pricing is continuing to rise," said Tobey Gonnerman, president of semiconductor distributor Fusion Worldwide.

"Consumers can expect to pay significantly higher prices for laptops, mobile phones, wearables and gaming devices very soon."

TrendForce said last year that Dell and Lenovo were planning price hikes of as much as 20% early in 2026.

Shares of Raspberry Pi, Xiaomi, Dell, HP Inc and Lenovo all fell in the last three months of 2025, with Xiaomi posting the biggest drop with a 27.2% decline.

HP CEO Enrique Lores said in November the company would raise PC prices due to "significant" memory chip costs, while Raspberry Pi CEO called the cost surge "painful" in a December blog post announcing price increases for its devices.

The weaker demand outlook could also hamper sales at electronics-focused retailers such as Best Buy, which had already warned last year that tariff-driven price increases could dissuade potential buyers.

Apple will report earnings on January 29, while Dell is slated to report on February 26. Xiaomi usually reports in late March.

APPLE’S MARKET POWER

Some analysts said Apple, with its scale, pricing power and deep supplier network, is better positioned to weather the memory chip price surge than its smaller rivals.

The company typically holds prices of its flagship iPhone lineup in the U.S. steady between its September launch events. Last year, it absorbed the hundreds of millions of dollars in tariff-related costs, instead of passing them on to customers.

"Apple is better-positioned, as it uses contract pricing (rather than more volatile spot pricing) for its purchases, securing better prices," Morningstar analyst William Kerwin said.

"But it isn’t immune, and may need to raise prices to pass on higher input costs."

title: Intel results to spotlight turnaround efforts as AI data centers boost chip demand
content:
By Arsheeya Bajwa

Jan 21 (Reuters) - Intel shareholders are optimistic about the company’s results like they have not been for many quarters, betting the turnaround CEO Lip-Bu Tan promised was taking root and that rapid data center build outs were fueling strong demand for its traditional server chips.

A slew of high-profile investments engineered by Tan last year piqued investor interest in a stock that had crashed in 2024 following years of management missteps, including a botched AI product roadmap that led to deep competitive losses and thousands of job cuts.

Intel’s stock gained 84% in 2025, far outperforming the benchmark semiconductor index’s 42% rise.

The company is slated to report fourth-quarter results after markets close on Thursday.

A $5  billion investment from Nvidia and $2  billion from SoftBank, alongside the U.S. government’s stake in the company, have strengthened Intel’s balance sheet and given Tan the flexibility to begin reshaping the company’s manufacturing and AI strategy.

Tan has also overhauled the company’s chipmaking operations and tightened what he said was a bloated management structure.

"It’s the most optimistic, I think, people have felt about the company in a long time; the near-term dynamic’s set up very well," said Ryuta Makino, analyst at Intel investor Gabelli Funds.

"That’s really the big Intel bull case here - I think there will be at least a double-digit server CPU (central processing unit) price hike in 2026."

At least 10 brokerages have raised their price targets or ratings for Intel over the last two months, indicating higher expectations from the company.

Intel is likely to report a more than 30% jump in its data center business to $4.43 billion for the quarter ended December, according to data compiled by LSEG.

The jump could be attributed to big tech companies building out advanced data centers that need Intel’s traditional server chips and CPUs alongside graphics processors made by the likes of Nvidia.

Sales in Intel’s personal computer unit likely rose 2.5% to $8.21 billion.

A LONG WAY TO GO

Intel has been consistently losing share in the PC market to rival AMD and chip blueprint designer Arm, and may now also face weaker PC demand as a global shortage of memory chips has boosted memory chip prices and made laptops more expensive.

"While we remain bullish on data center demand, we believe PC demand may moderate from increasing memory pricing, given memory accounts for 25% to 30% of PC bill of materials," UBS analysts said in a note earlier this month.

The brokerage expects a drop of 4% in global 2026 PC shipments, compared with the over 3% growth it projected earlier.

Intel’s refreshed product lineup may help plug some losses.

The company has started shipping its new "Panther Lake" PC chips - the first product made using Intel’s make-or-break 18A manufacturing technology. Its prior-generation PC chips were largely made by chip contractor TSMC.

Intel has long been its own largest manufacturing customer, but with its growing political goodwill, the Street is hoping for new foundry clients.

"We really like Lip-Bu Tan, but more importantly – more powerful people like President Trump, Secretary Lutnick, (Nvidia CEO) Jensen Huang and even (AMD CEO) Lisa Su like him even more as a business partner," Melius Research analysts said in a note.

Reuters has reported that Nvidia and Broadcom have run manufacturing tests with Intel, but much remains uncertain as only a small percentage of the chips printed via 18A have been good enough to make available to customers.

Intel has said its yields, or the number of good chips per silicon wafer, are improving monthly.

Pressured by poor yields, Intel’s adjusted gross margin is expected to have dropped about 6 percentage points to 36.5% in the December quarter.

title: KeyBanc maintains AMD stock rating, cites strong server demand through 2026
content:
KeyBanc projects AMD’s AI GPU revenue will reach $14-15 billion in 2026, supported by approximately 200,000 MI355 shipments in the first half of the year and 400,000-500,000 MI450 units in the second half, including about 290,000-300,000 for Helios racks. The firm expects MI455 GPUs to begin volume production in the third quarter of 2026, with volume rack shipments of Helios expected in the fourth quarter.

This 10-year agreement includes an initial deployment of 25 MW of critical IT load capacity, with the possibility of expanding to 200 MW. Additionally, Goldman Sachs expressed a preference for Broadcom and Nvidia over AMD in the compute ecosystem, focusing on sustainable AI capital expenditure and advancements in networking technology. These recent developments indicate AMD’s ongoing efforts to strengthen its position in the technology sector.

title: AMD appoints former Accenture CFO KC McClure to board of directors
content:
SANTA CLARA, Calif. - AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) has appointed KC McClure to its board of directors, the company announced Tuesday in a press release. The semiconductor giant, currently valued at $377 billion, has seen its shares surge nearly 91% over the past year.

McClure most recently served as a senior advisor to Accenture and was the company’s Chief Financial Officer from 2019 to 2024. During her 37-year career at Accenture, she held various finance and accounting leadership positions, including overseeing financial operations and investor relations.

"KC is a proven finance leader whose operating expertise and global perspective will be an asset as we execute our strategy," said Dr. Lisa Su, AMD chair and CEO.

McClure holds a Bachelor of Science degree in accounting and business administration from Pennsylvania State University’s Smeal College of Business. She currently serves on the university’s Smeal College of Business Board of Visitors and on the board of Goldman Sachs.

AMD, a semiconductor company that produces processors and related technologies, focuses on high-performance and AI computing solutions. The company’s products are used in cloud infrastructure, embedded systems, PCs and gaming devices.

In other recent news, Riot Platforms has secured a significant data center lease agreement with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at its Rockdale site in Texas. The agreement involves an initial deployment of 25 MW of critical IT load capacity, with the potential to expand up to 200 MW. Piper Sandler has reiterated an Overweight rating on Riot Platforms, maintaining a $26.00 price target. This development marks Riot Platforms’ first AI/HPC deal with AMD, showcasing a strategic move into the AI infrastructure space.

Meanwhile, AMD continues to attract attention in the investment community. Wells Fargo has reiterated its Overweight rating on AMD, with a price target of $345.00, citing significant upside potential. RBC Capital has initiated coverage of AMD with a Sector Perform rating and a price target of $230.00. The firm highlights AMD’s progress in the AI GPU market, particularly its partnership with OpenAI. In other developments, Goldman Sachs has expressed a preference for Broadcom and Nvidia within the compute ecosystem, noting their positions as beneficiaries of advancements in networking technology.

This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

title: AMD appoints former Accenture CFO KC McClure to board
content:
AMD (AMD) appointed KC McClure to its board of directors, the company announced Monday.

McClure served as Chief Financial Officer of Accenture from 2019 to 2024 and worked at the consulting firm for more than 37 years in various finance and accounting leadership positions. She oversaw financial operations and investor relations during her tenure and most recently worked as a senior advisor to Accenture.

"KC is a proven finance leader whose operating expertise and global perspective will be an asset as we execute our strategy and extend AMD’s leadership in high-performance and AI computing," said Dr. Lisa Su, AMD chair and CEO.

McClure holds a Bachelor of Science degree in accounting and business administration from Pennsylvania State University’s Smeal College of Business. She currently serves on the Smeal College of Business Board of Visitors and on the board of Goldman Sachs.

The appointment adds financial expertise to AMD’s board as the semiconductor company continues to compete in high-performance computing and artificial intelligence markets.

title: Goldman prefers Broadcom and Nvidia in compute ecosystem
content:
Investing.com - Goldman Sachs has reaffirmed its preference for Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) and Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) within the compute ecosystem, citing their ties to sustainable AI capital expenditure and positioning as beneficiaries of networking technology advancements.

The investment bank constructed an "inference cost curve" to compare different chip vendors’ solutions and their competitiveness over time, addressing investor questions about the GPU versus ASIC debate in artificial intelligence computing.

Goldman’s analysis indicates that Google/Broadcom’s TPU is rapidly closing the performance gap with Nvidia’s GPU solutions on compute cost performance, with TPU v7 achieving approximately 70% reduction in cost per token compared to TPU v6, putting it on par or slightly better than Nvidia’s GB200 NVL72 in absolute cost terms.

In other recent news, Riot Platforms has announced a significant development by securing a 10-year Data Center Lease and Services Agreement with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at its Rockdale site in Texas. This agreement involves an initial deployment of 25 MW of critical IT load capacity, with potential expansion to 200 MW, and is set to begin in January 2026. Piper Sandler has reiterated an Overweight rating on Riot Platforms, with a price target of $26.00, following this agreement. Meanwhile, AMD continues to receive attention from analysts, as Wells Fargo reiterated its Overweight rating and set a $345.00 price target, making it their top stock pick for 2026 due to its AI potential.

RBC Capital has initiated coverage on AMD with a Sector Perform rating and a price target of $230.00, highlighting its progress in the AI GPU market and a recent partnership with OpenAI. However, AMD shares, along with Nvidia’s, experienced a dip after OpenAI announced a multibillion-dollar partnership with AI chip startup Cerebras. This partnership with Cerebras, valued at more than $10 billion, aims to integrate advanced AI systems into OpenAI’s infrastructure through 2028. These developments reflect the dynamic landscape of partnerships and strategic moves within the tech industry.

title: Top 3 AI Stocks Dominating the Market, According to WarrenAI
content:
Investing.com -- The artificial intelligence revolution continues to reshape the investment landscape, with certain companies positioned to capture outsized returns. According to a recent analysis by WarrenAI, three companies stand out as the most promising AI investments based on their market position, financial performance, and growth potential.

NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA)

The undisputed leader in AI chips maintains its crown with an impressive $4.5 trillion market cap and over 80% share of the AI infrastructure market. NVIDIA’s dominance is reflected in its exceptional 63.8% EBITDA margin and projected revenue growth from $26.97 billion in 2023 to $253.36 billion by 2030.

The company’s CUDA ecosystem and full-stack AI approach create significant switching costs for customers, reinforcing its competitive moat. Despite a 35.3% one-year total return, analysts maintain a Strong Buy consensus with price targets suggesting up to 45.7% additional upside.

WarrenAI describes NVIDIA as the "AI king" with "top-tier margins" that still has "room to run."

Broadcom Inc (NASDAQ:AVGO)

As an AI ASIC powerhouse and networking leader, Broadcom has secured its position through strategic relationships with tech giants including Google, Meta, and ByteDance. The company boasts a $1.7 trillion market cap, delivers impressive 77% gross margins, and has achieved 28% revenue growth.

Analysts project Broadcom will double its AI semiconductor revenues within the next two years. With a 49.5% one-year total return and analyst price targets indicating 33.2% potential upside, the market consensus remains Overweight/Outperform.

WarrenAI highlights Broadcom as an "AI ASIC leader" that is both "profitable" with "strong upside" and "more diversified than it looks."

Advanced Micro Devices Inc (NASDAQ:AMD)

The challenger with momentum, AMD has leveraged its OpenAI partnership, new Ryzen AI chips, and record-high market share to deliver a remarkable 90.9% one-year return.

With a market cap of $377.43 billion, AMD is positioned to capture double-digit AI accelerator market share in a total addressable market expected to exceed $1 trillion by 2030. Despite a lower 20% EBITDA margin compared to its larger competitors, analysts maintain a Strong Buy consensus with price targets suggesting up to 52.3% upside.

WarrenAI identifies AMD as the "fastest grower" with an "innovation pipeline" that is "benefiting as NVIDIA’s only real challenger in high-performance AI."

These three companies represent the current leaders in the AI chip space, each with unique strengths and growth trajectories that have attracted significant investor attention.

title: South Korea says US chip tariff to have limited immediate impact
content:
SEOUL, Jan 17 (Reuters) - South Korea’s trade minister said on Saturday that a U.S. proclamation imposing a 25% tariff on certain advanced computing chips would have a limited impact on South Korean companies.

"While the government remains cautious at an early stage, the first-phase measures announced so far focus on advanced chips made by Nvidia and AMD," Trade Minister Yeo Han-koo said.

"Since the memory chips that South Korean companies mainly export are currently excluded, the immediate impact is expected to be limited."

Yeo cautioned, however, that it was "not yet time to be reassured," noting uncertainty over when and how a potential second phase could be expanded.

He added that the government would continue to work closely with industry to seek the best possible outcome for South Korean companies.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Wednesday signed a proclamation to address national security concerns related to semiconductor imports, imposing a 25% tariff on certain artificial intelligence chips, such as Nvidia’s H200 AI processor and AMD’s MI325X.

The White House said the tariffs would be narrowly focused and would not apply to chips and derivative devices imported for U.S. data centers - a huge consumer of AI chips - startups, non-data center consumer applications, non-data center civil industrial applications and U.S. public sector applications.

The United States, in the near future, may also impose broader tariffs on imports of semiconductors and their derivative products to incentivise domestic manufacturing, according to the fact sheet.

U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said on Friday that South Korean chipmakers and Taiwanese companies that are not investing in the U.S. may face up to 100% tariffs unless they commit to increased production on American soil, Bloomberg reported. Lutnick was at a groundbreaking ceremony for Micron’s new plant outside Syracuse, New York.

The proclamation follows a nine-month investigation under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 and targets a number of high-end semiconductors meeting certain performance benchmarks and devices containing them for import duties. The action is part of a broader effort to create incentives for chipmakers to produce more semiconductors in the U.S. and decrease reliance on chip manufacturers in places like Taiwan.

title: Riot Platforms stock rises as Piper Sandler reiterates Overweight rating
content:
Investing.com - Riot Platforms (NASDAQ:RIOT) has signed its first AI/HPC deal with AMD at its Rockdale site near Austin, Texas, according to a Piper Sandler research note. AMD (NASDAQ:AMD), currently trading at $232.50, has seen impressive momentum with a 12.18% return over the past week and 92.43% over the last year.

AMD also holds the right of first refusal on an additional 100 MW of critical IT capacity, potentially bringing its total commitment to 200 MW of critical IT infrastructure.

Piper Sandler views the deal as "an encouraging first step towards RIOT eventually contracting all ~1.2 GW critical IT across Rockdale & Corsicana" and believes it establishes Riot Platforms as a "credible data center option for hyperscaler tenants."

The research firm estimates that a full transition of these sites to AI/HPC data centers could represent over $30 per share in value for Riot Platforms stock based on management’s run rate NOI projections.

In other recent news, Riot Platforms announced a significant development by securing a data center lease agreement with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The 10-year agreement will see an initial deployment of 25 MW of critical IT load capacity at Riot’s Rockdale Site in Texas, with the potential to expand to 200 MW by May 2026. Meanwhile, AMD remains a focal point for analysts, as Wells Fargo reiterated its Overweight rating and set a $345.00 price target, citing the company’s potential in artificial intelligence (AI) as a key factor. RBC Capital also initiated coverage of AMD with a Sector Perform rating and a price target of $230.00, acknowledging AMD’s progress in the AI GPU market and its partnership with OpenAI.

Additionally, AMD shares faced some pressure after OpenAI announced a substantial computing partnership with AI chip startup Cerebras Systems. The partnership, reportedly valued at over $10 billion, includes OpenAI’s purchase of up to 750 megawatts of computing power over three years. This move may impact AMD as OpenAI plans to integrate Cerebras-designed chips into its infrastructure, potentially reducing its reliance on AMD and Nvidia hardware. These developments highlight the dynamic landscape in which AMD operates, with strategic partnerships and analyst ratings playing a crucial role in shaping its future.

title: Riot Platforms stock rating reiterated at Buy by BTIG on data center potential
content:
The research firm highlighted AMD’s expansion option for 75MW and right of first refusal on an additional 100MW of IT load at Riot’s facilities. Based on initial deal terms, these expansions could represent approximately $2.2 billion in revenue over a 10-year period, though pricing might differ due to the larger scale of subsequent buildouts. This potential revenue stream is significant for Riot, which reported $637 million in revenue over the last twelve months and remains profitable with earnings per share of $0.49.

BTIG noted that while Riot has secured a high-performance computing (HPC) customer at its Rockdale location, the firm has always viewed Riot’s Corsicana site outside Dallas as a more attractive potential HPC data center location. The Corsicana site features approximately 1GW of power infrastructure capacity on land already owned by the company.

According to BTIG’s channel checks, the Corsicana location is considered one of the most attractive large-scale data center locations in the United States, adding to Riot’s potential value proposition.

With Rockdale land now owned and soon generating HPC revenue, BTIG emphasized that Riot has two "blue chip" data center locations with roughly 1GW of additional IT capacity to be contracted, excluding IT load on options.

In other recent news, Riot Platforms has been active with several significant developments. The company reported producing 460 bitcoin in December 2025, an 8% increase from November, though 11% lower than the same month in 2024. Riot Platforms sold 1,818 bitcoin during December, bringing in net proceeds of $161.6 million. Additionally, Riot Platforms announced a 10-year data center lease agreement with Advanced Micro Devices at its Rockdale Site in Texas, with an initial deployment of 25 MW of critical IT load capacity. In connection with this, Riot acquired 200 acres of land at the Rockdale site for $96 million, funded through the sale of approximately 1,080 bitcoin. Piper Sandler has maintained an Overweight rating on Riot Platforms, highlighting it as a "sleeping giant" with a price target of $26.00. Moreover, Riot Platforms announced a CFO transition, with Colin Yee stepping down on March 1, 2026, to be succeeded by Jason Chung. The company emphasized that this transition is not due to any disputes regarding financial practices.

title: Riot stock surges after securing AMD data center lease deal
content:
Investing.com -- Riot Platforms (NASDAQ:RIOT) stock surged over 7% Friday morning after the Bitcoin mining company announced it has secured a data center lease agreement with Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD).

The company revealed it has signed a 10-year Data Center Lease and Services Agreement with AMD at its Rockdale Site in Texas. The deal includes an initial deployment of 25 MW of critical IT load capacity to be delivered in phases beginning in January 2026 and completing in May 2026, with potential expansion to 200 MW.

Riot expects the agreement to generate approximately $311 million in contract revenue over the initial term. If AMD exercises all three five-year extension options, total contract revenue could reach approximately $1 billion.

"This partnership represents a validation of Riot’s infrastructure, development capabilities, the attractiveness of our sites, our readily available power capacity, and our ability to offer innovative solutions to meet the requirements of top-tier tenants," said Jason Les, CEO of Riot.

In conjunction with the lease agreement, Riot also announced it has completed the fee simple acquisition of 200 acres of land at its Rockdale Site for $96 million. The purchase was funded entirely by selling approximately 1,080 bitcoin from the company’s balance sheet.

The Rockdale Site features a 700 MW interconnection to the grid, dedicated water supply, and fiber connectivity. With this acquisition, Riot now owns and manages over 1,100 acres and 1.7 GW of power capacity across its two Texas facilities.

AMD’s CIO Hasmukh Ranjan commented, "At AMD, advancing high-performance computing and AI requires partners that can match our pace and scale. We’re excited to work with Riot, whose capabilities, power availability, and high-density solutions align with our infrastructure roadmap."

The initial deployment will leverage Riot’s existing infrastructure, with the company commencing retrofitting of an existing building at a cost of $89.8 million.

title: Microchip, Analog Devices target raised ahead of semiconductor earnings
content:
Investing.com --Wolfe Research raised estimates on select semiconductor names ahead of the fourth-quarter earnings season, arguing that while demand visibility remains mixed across end markets, downside risks appear limited and company-specific drivers are becoming more important.

Wolfe said commentary from analog chipmakers is likely to be mixed but stable, with no broad-based deterioration expected.

The firm expects Microchip Technology to sound the most bullish, following two positive pre-announcements this quarter, noting that Microchip’s recovery has lagged peers and is now gaining momentum.

Wolfe raised its price target on Microchip to $90 from $76 citing stronger-than-expected December-quarter results and management’s commitment to well-above-seasonal growth in the March quarter.

Wolfe also lifted its price target on Analog Devices to $330 by $30, pointing to company-specific drivers and multiple expansion across the analog group.

Wolfe said firms such as Texas Instruments and NXP Semiconductors, which exited the downturn earlier, are likely to wait for firmer demand signals before calling a sustained recovery.

Wolfe said Monolithic Power Systems in analog remains its preferred name due to high exposure to artificial intelligence, while Texas Instruments remains attractive longer term as its multi-year capital expenditure cycle winds down and free cash flow improves.

Wolfe has a positive long-term view on Advanced Micro Devices, raising forecasts on expectations that AI will drive earnings power beyond $10 per share starting in the second half of 2026.

Though near-term catalysts are limited and there are concern PC demand if memory prices rise. Wolfe expects Intel to post acceptable near-term results on server demand, but sees continued market share losses to AMD.

Wolfe sees near-term upside driven by strong iPhone sales, but trimmed estimates for Qualcomm, citing higher operating expenses following the Alphawave acquisition and longer-term share losses at Samsung.

title: Chip equipment stocks soar after TSMC’s bullish forecast
content:
Investing.com -- Chip equipment stocks led a sector-wide rally Thursday after Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (NYSE:TSM) announced a significant increase in capital expenditure plans and a robust revenue growth forecast for 2026.

Lam Research (NASDAQ:LRCX) shares jumped 7%, Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) surged 8%, KLA Corporation (NASDAQ:KLAC) climbed 6%, and Teradyne (NASDAQ:TER) rose 3% following TSMC’s announcement. The world’s largest contract chipmaker forecasted capital expenditure for 2026 of $52 billion to $56 billion, substantially exceeding analysts’ consensus estimate of approximately $46 billion.

TSMC also indicated that its capital expenditure would be "significantly higher" over the next three years compared to the $101 billion spent over the past three years. Additionally, the company projected revenue growth of close to 30% in 2026.

The positive outlook from TSMC rippled through the broader semiconductor sector. NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) and AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) both gained 1%, while Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) rose 2%. Memory chip manufacturers also benefited, with Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) climbing 3%, Western Digital (NASDAQ:WDC) advancing 3.6%, and Seagate (NASDAQ:STX) adding 2%.

TSMC’s aggressive investment plans signal strong confidence in continued demand for advanced semiconductor manufacturing capacity, particularly as artificial intelligence applications drive the need for more sophisticated chips.

title: Wells Fargo upgrades key chip names; AMD a top pick
content:
Investing.com -- Wells Fargo issued a broad wave of semiconductor upgrades as part of its 2026 outlook, arguing that the sector remains positioned for further gains despite strong year-to-date performance.

The firm highlighted AI demand indicators, a token-driven supply and demand model, and wafer-fab-equipment (WFE) forecasts as central inputs.

Wells Fargo named AMD its top pick, citing an “expanding multi-GW MI450X pipeline, server CPU share gains, & embedded recovery.”

The firm also upgraded Broadcom to Overweight with a $430 price target, saying concerns around gross margins are “overdone” and pointing to “Google TPU upside + significant additional custom XPU ramps.”

Wells Fargo also maintained a positive view on what it called the “memory super-cycle,” noting Micron is on track to “+$40/sh EPS,” while SanDisk is projected to move “to >$30/sh EPS.”

In semiconductor capital equipment, Applied Materials remains the firm’s “top semi cap pick in 2026,” but Wells Fargo also upgraded Lam Research and KLA to Overweight.

Despite the group being “a consensus long,” the firm said that for AMAT, tightening supply-demand dynamics support “a WFE acceleration in 2027,” prompting it to raise estimates by an average of 10 percent.

Analog names received similar treatment, with upgrades to Overweight for Analog Devices and Monolithic Power Systems. Wells Fargo said sentiment is improving given “relatively easy y/y comps” and the potential for inventory replenishment.

The bank now forecasts 2026 semiconductor revenue of $1.02 trillion, up 29 percent year over year, with AI infrastructure expected to drive massive long-term investment.
